intro_text:
Radhanagar Beach is a crescent of pale, fine-grained sand about two kilometres long on the west coast of Havelock Island, one of the larger inhabited islands of the Andaman and Nicobar archipelago in the Bay of Bengal. Locally it is still called Beach No. 7, the number the colonial-era survey gave it. It sits roughly 11 to 12 kilometres from the Havelock jetty, with a wall of dense tropical forest running the length of the sand at its back and open ocean in front. In 2004 TIME magazine named it the best beach in Asia, and that single sentence has carried it onto nearly every Indian beach list since.

The geography is what makes the sunsets. Radhanagar faces almost due west across the Bay of Bengal with no significant landmass between it and the Indian mainland, so the sun drops straight into open water rather than behind a headland. It is a west-facing beach on an east-coast country, which is rarer than it sounds and is the whole reason the light works.

The beach is also genuinely undeveloped. There are no hotels, bars, or shops on the sand itself — the resorts and guesthouses are set back in the forest and along the road, and construction near the shore is deliberately restricted. What you get on the beach is sand, trees, and water, which is part of why it reads as wild even on a busy day. It is also why there is very little to do here after dark; the beach effectively closes at sunset.

The part the rankings leave out is dated and specific. On 29 April 2010 Lauren Failla, a 25-year-old American visitor from New Jersey, was killed by a saltwater crocodile while snorkelling off Radhanagar; her body was recovered the next day on a neighbouring beach. Saltwater crocodiles live in the mangrove creeks of these islands and occasionally move along the open coast, and the death forced a reckoning the brochures never mention — warning signs, intermittent beach closures, and forest-department monitoring that continues today.

Getting here is part of the cost. Havelock — officially renamed Swaraj Dweep in December 2018 after Subhas Chandra Bose — is reached by ferry from Port Blair, about 37 nautical miles away, a crossing of one to three hours depending on the boat. For roughly four months a year, when the southwest monsoon roughens the sea, ferries run reduced or stop, the surf turns unfriendly, and the beach that won the award is a different and emptier place.
spike_explainer:
Radhanagar's claim rests on one line that has been repeated for two decades: in 2004 TIME magazine called it the best beach in Asia. That is the engine of its fame, and it is worth being precise about what it is and is not.

TIME's recognition came in a 2004 feature on the best of Asia, not from a coastal scientist's annual survey. It is an editorial judgement, the kind of one-time pick that gets frozen into tourism copy and quoted as though it were a standing measurement. Wikipedia, drawing on the same period, phrases it more cautiously as one of the best beaches in the world. The exact wording shifts between sources — "best in Asia," "one of the best on Earth" — and the honest version is that a major Western magazine singled out a remote Indian beach once, in 2004, and the label stuck. It has never been re-awarded. Almost everything written about Radhanagar since is downstream of that one citation.

**What the award was actually pointing at is a specific, defensible piece of geography.** Radhanagar faces west across the open Bay of Bengal from the west coast of Havelock, with effectively nothing between the beach and the Indian mainland for hundreds of kilometres. India is an east-leaning country — its most famous beaches in Goa, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu mostly face the Arabian Sea or the Bay from the mainland, where the sun rises over the water and sets behind land. Radhanagar inverts that. The sun goes down directly into the sea, with no island or headland to clip the horizon, and the pale, fine sand holds the amber-to-turquoise gradient in the last light. The sunset is not a bonus; it is the structural feature the ranking was reacting to.

**The second thing the award was pointing at is an absence: there is no commercial strip on the beach.** Unlike almost every famous beach that gets a magazine ranking and then gets a row of bars behind it, Radhanagar has stayed bare. Construction near the shoreline is restricted, the accommodation sits back in the forest, and the sand itself carries no permanent buildings. The forest comes almost to the high-water line, two kilometres of it, dense and humid and audible at dusk. That undeveloped backdrop is rare for a beach this celebrated, and it is the reason photographs of Radhanagar look like a coastline rather than a resort frontage.

**But the same wildness that the award flattered is the reason a person died here.** This is the hard centre of Radhanagar's story. The Andaman Islands are home to the saltwater crocodile, Crocodylus porosus — the largest living reptile, an animal that lives in the mangrove creeks and estuaries of the archipelago and is entirely capable of moving along the open coast. On 29 April 2010, Lauren Failla, a 25-year-old American snorkelling off Radhanagar, was taken and killed by one. Her body was found the next day on an adjacent beach. A U.S. State Department spokesman noted at the time that roughly two dozen crocodile attacks, four of them fatal, had been recorded in the territory over the previous 25 years. The death prompted demands for trained lifeguards and prompted the local administration to confront a tension it had been ignoring: the same forest-and-mangrove wilderness that makes the Andamans extraordinary is crocodile country, and a beach marketed as a tropical idyll sits inside it. Warning signs went up, the beach has been closed at times after sightings, and the forest department now monitors the coast — but no fence separates the award-winning sand from the sea the crocodile came out of.

**Radhanagar's third defining fact is that it is genuinely hard to reach, and that remoteness is load-bearing.** Havelock is not on the Indian mainland and not even the main Andaman island; it is reached by ferry from Port Blair, about 37 nautical miles across open water, a one-to-three-hour crossing. For roughly four months a year the southwest monsoon roughens the Bay, ferry schedules are cut or suspended, and the surf at Radhanagar makes swimming inadvisable. Day-trippers who come over and back from Port Blair miss the sunset entirely, because the boats leave before the light that the beach is famous for. The effort filters the crowd, but it also means the beach you read about — calm water, clear amber sunset — exists for only part of the year. The Radhanagar of November to February and the Radhanagar of the monsoon are, in practice, two different beaches.

That is the argument. Radhanagar is a west-facing, undeveloped, two-kilometre crescent on a remote forested island, picked once by TIME in 2004 and coasting on that line ever since. The west-facing geometry is real and gives it a sunset most Indian beaches cannot. The bare, forested backdrop is real and rare. And the wildness those qualities depend on is the same wildness that put a saltwater crocodile in the water in April 2010 — which is the part of the singular beach that the singular sentence about it never includes.
honest_reckoning_note: The single hardest fact about Radhanagar is that on 29 April 2010 a saltwater crocodile killed Lauren Failla, a 25-year-old American visitor, while she snorkelled off the beach; her body was recovered the following day on a neighbouring shore. Crocodylus porosus lives in the mangrove creeks of the Andamans and moves along the open coast, and a U.S. official said at the time that about two dozen attacks, four fatal, had been recorded in the territory over 25 years. The beach has since carried warning signs and intermittent closures, and the forest department monitors the coast, but the award-winning sand and the crocodile's water are the same place. The wider context is heavier still. These islands sit on a fault line: the 2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami killed at least 1,310 people in the Andaman and Nicobar territory with thousands more missing. And the Andamans' fame as a beach destination is built next to some of the most vulnerable Indigenous peoples on Earth — the Jarawa, exposed to exploitative "human safaris" along the trunk road despite court orders, and the Sentinelese of North Sentinel Island, a no-contact tribe whose reserve is illegal to enter, both lacking immunity to outside disease. None of that is a reason not to come, but a beach this remote is never just a beach, and Radhanagar's beauty is inseparable from the fragility and the danger of where it is.
reckoning_pullquote: TIME called Radhanagar the best beach in Asia in 2004; six years later a saltwater crocodile killed a snorkeller in the same water, and the brochures still don't say so.

key_facts.label: TIME magazine, 2004
key_facts.value: Named by TIME in 2004 as the best beach in Asia (Wikipedia phrases it as among the best in the world); an editorial pick, never re-awarded, and the root of nearly all its fame

key_facts.label: Length and setting
key_facts.value: About a 2 km crescent of pale, fine sand on the west coast of Havelock Island, backed by dense tropical forest, with no buildings on the beach itself

key_facts.label: Also called Beach No. 7
key_facts.value: Locally known as Beach No. 7, the number from the colonial-era island survey; about 11-12 km from the Havelock jetty

key_facts.label: 2010 crocodile death
key_facts.value: On 29 April 2010 Lauren Failla, 25, an American from New Jersey, was killed by a saltwater crocodile while snorkelling; her body was found the next day on a nearby beach

key_facts.label: Crocodile attack record
key_facts.value: A U.S. State Department spokesman said in 2010 that about 24 crocodile attacks, 4 of them fatal, had been recorded in the territory over the previous 25 years

key_facts.label: Havelock renamed Swaraj Dweep
key_facts.value: Havelock Island was officially renamed Swaraj Dweep in December 2018 after Subhas Chandra Bose; Ross and Neil islands were renamed at the same time

key_facts.label: 2004 tsunami toll
key_facts.value: The 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami killed at least 1,310 people in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, with about 5,600 more missing
key_facts.source: Wikipedia: Effect of the 2004 Indian Ocean earthquake on India

key_facts.label: Access and monsoon
key_facts.value: Reached by ferry from Port Blair, ~37 nautical miles; for roughly 4 months in the southwest monsoon ferries are cut and the surf makes swimming inadvisable

margin_notes.text: "Beach No. 7" is not a nickname; it is the survey number from the colonial mapping of Havelock. TIME's 2004 pick gave it the Radhanagar fame, but locals and ferry crews still say Beach 7.

India mostly faces its sunsets onto land. Radhanagar faces west across open Bay of Bengal with nothing between it and the mainland, so the sun sets into the sea. That west-facing geometry is the whole reason the light works.

No bars, no shops, no hotels on the sand — construction near the shore is restricted, so the accommodation sits back in the forest. Rare for a beach this famous, and the reason it photographs as coast, not resort.

29 April 2010: a saltwater crocodile killed an American snorkeller here. Crocodylus porosus lives in the islands' mangrove creeks and ranges the coast. Warning signs and closures followed; no fence separates the sand from the water it came from.

Havelock became Swaraj Dweep in December 2018, after Subhas Chandra Bose. Maps and tickets use both names. The crossing from Port Blair is real open water; in the monsoon the boats stop and the beach empties out.

day_in_time.dawn: At first light Radhanagar is near-empty; the day-trippers are still on the morning ferry from Port Blair. The forest at the back of the sand is loud with birds before the heat sets in, and the water is at its calmest. The early calm is also when the forest department and any posted guards watch the shore most closely.
day_in_time.midday: By late morning the beach has filled with visitors who came over from Havelock village, and the tropical sun is unforgiving on a beach with no shade except the treeline at the back. Swimming is best in the calm season; in the monsoon months the surf turns the water rough and swimming is advised against. There are no permanent lifeguards across most of the day.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon is what the beach is for. The sun drops straight into the open Bay of Bengal with no land to clip it, throwing the amber-on-turquoise gradient across the pale sand that put Radhanagar on the 2004 list. Day-trippers who must catch the return ferry to Port Blair leave before this, and miss the entire point of the place.
day_in_time.night: After dark Radhanagar effectively closes. There is no commercial strip on the sand, no lighting, and the forest behind it is genuinely wild — the accommodation is set back along the road. This is not a beach to be on at night, both because there is nothing here and because the coast is monitored crocodile habitat.

food_drink.name: No food on the beach itself
food_drink.description: Radhanagar has no restaurants, bars, or kiosks on the sand — the restriction on shoreline development that keeps it bare also keeps it food-free. A few seasonal vendors and coconut sellers sometimes work the entrance and car-park area, but you eat in the forest or back in Havelock village, not on the beach.
food_drink.where: Beach entrance / car-park area, when present

food_drink.name: Andaman seafood and fish curry
food_drink.description: Havelock's cooking leans on the day's catch — grilled and curried fish, prawns, crab and squid, served with rice in the guesthouses and small restaurants of the village and along the road inland from the beach. Most produce other than seafood is shipped in from the mainland, which is reflected in the prices on a remote island.
food_drink.where: Havelock village and guesthouses inland from Radhanagar

food_drink.name: Coconut water and fresh fruit
food_drink.description: Tender coconut sold from the entrance and roadside is the standard drink in the heat, alongside imported fruit. Alcohol is limited and resort-priced on Havelock; this is not a beach with a bar scene, and the dry, undeveloped sand is part of why it stayed on the rankings.
food_drink.where: Roadside stalls near the beach and in Havelock village

timeline.title: TIME magazine names it the best beach in Asia
timeline.description: TIME's 2004 best-of-Asia coverage singled out Radhanagar, the citation that has driven nearly all of the beach's subsequent fame. It was an editorial, one-time pick rather than a recurring scientific survey, and has never been re-awarded; sources vary between 'best in Asia' and 'among the best in the world.'

timeline.title: Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami strike the Andamans
timeline.description: On 26 December 2004 the magnitude-9 Indian Ocean earthquake and the tsunami that followed struck the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, which lie just north of the epicentre. At least 1,310 people were killed in the territory with about 5,600 more missing; some islands were submerged and reshaped.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Effect of the 2004 Indian Ocean earthquake on India; Britannica

timeline.title: American snorkeller killed by a saltwater crocodile
timeline.description: On 29 April 2010 Lauren Failla, a 25-year-old American from New Jersey, was killed by a saltwater crocodile while snorkelling off Radhanagar. Her body was recovered the next day on a neighbouring beach. The death drew international coverage and demands for lifeguards at Andaman tourist beaches.

timeline.title: Crocodile warnings and the tourism-vs-sanctuary debate
timeline.description: In the weeks after the attack, conservationists noted that the Andamans' mangroves are core saltwater-crocodile habitat and that the islands had to choose between marketing themselves as a beach destination and acknowledging that they are also crocodile country. Warning signs and intermittent closures followed at Radhanagar.

timeline.title: Court orders restrict Jarawa 'human safaris'
timeline.description: Through the early 2010s, Indian courts and authorities issued orders restricting tourist traffic and 'human safaris' along the Andaman Trunk Road that runs through the Jarawa tribal reserve, after reporting exposed tourists treating the Indigenous Jarawa as a roadside attraction. Enforcement has remained uneven.

timeline.title: Havelock renamed Swaraj Dweep
timeline.description: In December 2018 the Indian government renamed Havelock Island as Swaraj Dweep in honour of Subhas Chandra Bose, who had raised the Indian flag at Port Blair in 1943. Ross Island became Netaji Subhash Bose Dweep and Neil Island became Shaheed Dweep in the same announcement.

timeline.title: Sentinelese killing renews tribal-isolation debate
timeline.description: In November 2018 an American visitor, John Allen Chau, was killed attempting to contact the Sentinelese of North Sentinel Island, the no-contact tribe whose reserve is illegal to enter. The case put the Andamans' Indigenous-protection ethics into global headlines and underlined how close mass tourism sits to vulnerable peoples.

timeline.title: North Sentinel reaffirmed as a closed tribal reserve
timeline.description: In February 2019 India's Ministry of Home Affairs reaffirmed that North Sentinel Island and the surrounding waters are a tribal reserve, making unauthorised travel illegal, with naval and coast-guard surveillance — a reminder that the same archipelago marketed for its beaches contains some of the most protected human communities on Earth.

zones.name: Central crescent
zones.character: The wide central sweep of pale sand backed by tropical forest and facing open water to the west. The main swimming and sunbathing stretch, and the spot the sunset photographs are taken from.
zones.best_for: Swimming in the calm season, the sunset view, walking the length of the sand
zones.notes: Best in the November-February window; in the monsoon the surf turns rough and swimming is advised against. No permanent lifeguards across most of the day.

zones.name: Forest backdrop and treeline
zones.character: The dense tropical forest that runs the full length of the beach at its back, almost to the high-water line. The only shade on the sand and the source of the birdsong that rises at dusk.
zones.best_for: Shade, birdlife, the wild backdrop that keeps the beach undeveloped
zones.notes: The forest comes right to the sand; the coast is monitored crocodile habitat, so don't treat the creek mouths and edges as a place to wade alone.

zones.name: Entrance and car-park area
zones.character: The access point where the road from Havelock village meets the beach, with the ticket entry, occasional coconut and snack vendors, and parking for cabs, autos and rented two-wheelers.
zones.best_for: Arriving, a drink before or after, the only commerce associated with the beach
zones.notes: There is no food on the sand itself; this entrance area is where any vendors are. The last return ferries to Port Blair leave before sunset.

landmarks.name: Radhanagar (Beach No. 7) crescent
landmarks.description: The roughly 2 km west-facing crescent of pale, fine sand backed by dense tropical forest. Its west-facing geometry across the open Bay of Bengal is what gives it the open-water sunset that the 2004 TIME pick was reacting to, and the restriction on shoreline building is what keeps it bare.

landmarks.name: Elephant Beach, Havelock
landmarks.description: A separate beach on Havelock reached by a forest trek or a short boat ride, known for its fringing reef and snorkelling. It is the usual second stop for visitors who come for Radhanagar, and a reminder that Havelock's coast is a mix of reef shallows and open mangrove-fed water.

landmarks.name: Cellular Jail, Port Blair
landmarks.description: The colonial-era prison in Port Blair, completed in 1906, where the British held Indian independence activists in solitary cells. It is the standard historic stop before the ferry to Havelock and the anchor of the islands' independence-era history, including Subhas Chandra Bose's 1943 visit.

cultural_refs.title: TIME magazine: best beach in Asia (2004)
cultural_refs.creator: TIME
cultural_refs.description: TIME's 2004 best-of-Asia recognition is the cultural touchstone for Radhanagar and is quoted on virtually every Indian travel listing of the beach. It is an editorial, one-time judgement rather than an objective measurement, and has never been re-awarded; the exact wording varies by source between 'best in Asia' and 'among the best in the world.'

cultural_refs.title: Renaming to Swaraj Dweep (2018)
cultural_refs.creator: Government of India
cultural_refs.description: The December 2018 renaming of Havelock to Swaraj Dweep, in honour of Subhas Chandra Bose, is itself a cultural and political statement about reclaiming colonial place-names; 'Havelock' commemorated a British general of the 1857 era. Both names remain in common use on maps and tickets.

cultural_refs.title: The Jarawa 'human safari' controversy
cultural_refs.description: Reporting through the 2010s on tourists treating the Indigenous Jarawa as a roadside spectacle along the Andaman Trunk Road became an international ethics story about the islands, prompting court restrictions. It is the uncomfortable cultural backdrop to the Andamans' rise as a beach destination.

recurring_events.name: Southwest monsoon
recurring_events.when_text: Roughly late May to September (southwest monsoon)
recurring_events.description: During the southwest monsoon the Bay of Bengal roughens, ferry schedules from Port Blair to Havelock are reduced or suspended, and the surf at Radhanagar makes swimming inadvisable. For about four months a year the award-winning beach is wetter, emptier, and a different place from the postcard.

recurring_events.name: Peak season
recurring_events.description: The dry months bring the calmest water, the lowest humidity, and the clearest sunsets, and with them the heaviest visitor traffic from Port Blair day-trippers and resort guests onto a single undeveloped beach. This is the window the rankings describe.

things_to_know.label: Saltwater crocodiles are real here
things_to_know.note: A saltwater crocodile killed a snorkeller off this beach on 29 April 2010. Crocodylus porosus lives in the islands' mangrove creeks and ranges the coast. Heed warning signs and any posted closures, don't wander into creek mouths or mangrove edges, and don't swim at dawn, dusk, or in murky water.

things_to_know.label: Monsoon changes everything
things_to_know.note: For roughly four months from late May the southwest monsoon roughens the sea, cuts or suspends the Port Blair-Havelock ferries, and makes swimming at Radhanagar inadvisable. November to February is the calm, clear window; plan around the boats, not just the beach.

things_to_know.label: Nothing on the sand, closes at dusk
things_to_know.note: There are no shops, bars, hotels, or reliable lifeguard cover on the beach itself, and it effectively closes after sunset. Bring water and sun cover, eat in Havelock village or your guesthouse, and note that the last return ferries to Port Blair leave before the famous sunset.

things_to_know.label: You are in a fragile, sensitive place
things_to_know.note: These islands sit on an active fault (the 2004 tsunami killed over 1,300 here) and are home to protected Indigenous peoples. Do not seek out the Jarawa along the trunk road or attempt to approach North Sentinel Island, which is a closed tribal reserve illegal to enter.

access_note: Radhanagar Beach is on the west coast of Havelock Island (officially Swaraj Dweep), about 11-12 km from the Havelock jetty and roughly 72 km from Port Blair, the territorial capital. There is no direct route: you fly into Veer Savarkar International Airport at Port Blair (IXZ), then take a ferry across about 37 nautical miles of the Bay of Bengal to Havelock, a crossing of roughly one to three hours depending on whether you take a government or faster private boat. From the Havelock jetty, reach the beach by cab, auto-rickshaw, or a rented two-wheeler in about 25-30 minutes. Crucially, for about four months in the southwest monsoon (roughly late May to September) ferry schedules are reduced or suspended and the surf makes swimming inadvisable; November to February is the calm season. Day-trippers who return to Port Blair by ferry the same day leave before sunset and miss the light the beach is known for. There is no food, accommodation, or dependable lifeguard service on the beach itself.
